content.text: A self-service prompt registry is a platform tool that allows teams to author, version, publish, and consume prompt templates through a centralized catalog without requiring manual platform team involvement in the publication workflow. Teams submit prompt templates through a CLI or web portal; the publication pipeline validates the prompt schema, runs a smoke eval, checks for prompt injection patterns, and on success publishes a versioned artifact to the registry. Consuming services retrieve prompts from the registry at runtime or at build time, pinned to a specific version, rather than embedding prompt strings directly in application code.

The registry stores not just the prompt text but the full context needed to use and evaluate the prompt: the input variable schema, the recommended model and inference parameters, the eval suite run ID that validated the prompt, and the list of active services currently using each version. This metadata makes the registry a living knowledge base of organizational prompt assets rather than just a string storage service.

Without a prompt registry, prompt templates live as strings embedded in application code: invisible to other teams, not versioned independently from the code that uses them, and difficult to audit or review for quality and safety. When a prompt change improves one service's performance, other services that might benefit from the same pattern have no way to discover it. When a prompt contains an injection vulnerability or a safety issue, there is no inventory to determine which services use it.

Self-service is specifically important because prompt authoring is high-frequency, iterative work. A team tuning a summarization prompt for their service might publish and deprecate a dozen versions in a week as they optimize phrasing. If each publication requires a platform team review ticket, the iteration speed drops dramatically. Self-service publication with automated quality gates provides the governance without the bottleneck.
Prompt versions are independently tracked, enabling rollback to a previous prompt without a code deployment.
Centralized registry enables discovery of existing prompts that can be reused or adapted rather than authored from scratch.
Automated safety scanning at publication prevents prompts with injection vulnerabilities from reaching production.
Eval score history per prompt version provides evidence that quality is stable or improving over time.
Services pinned to registry versions receive a clear upgrade path when new versions are published.
Audit trail of who published each prompt version, when, and with what eval score satisfies compliance requirements.
Adding a network dependency for prompt retrieval introduces a failure mode: if the registry is unavailable, services cannot retrieve prompts at runtime.
Schema validation for prompt variables adds authoring overhead compared to free-form string templates.
Teams may game the smoke eval to pass publication gates without actually achieving good quality.
Registry versions can proliferate if teams publish frequently without pruning deprecated versions.
Multi-language support requires separate client SDKs for Python and JavaScript, doubling maintenance work.

Authors write prompt templates using a lightweight template language such as Jinja2 or Handlebars that references named input variables. The prompt definition includes a schema section specifying which variables are required, their types, and their descriptions. Authors publish through the platform CLI or web portal, specifying the prompt name, a version string following semantic versioning, and an optional change description. The publication pipeline runs automatically: it validates the schema, executes a configurable smoke eval suite against the new prompt version, runs a static analysis pass to detect common injection patterns, and if all checks pass, stores the versioned prompt artifact in the registry.

Consuming services retrieve prompts using a thin SDK that accepts a prompt name and version range. The SDK fetches the prompt from the registry, caches it for the service's lifetime with optional hot-reload on version changes, and provides a render method that applies the variable substitution. The SDK also validates that the caller is providing all required variables and that variable values match the expected types, catching integration errors before the prompt reaches the LLM API.

Version management follows semantic versioning semantics. Patch versions may only change whitespace, punctuation, or formatting. Minor versions may add new optional variables or rephrase existing instructions without changing the semantic intent. Major versions may change input variables, significantly restructure the prompt, or change the recommended model. Services pinned to a minor version range receive minor updates automatically; major versions require explicit upgrade.

Introduce a prompt registry when multiple teams are maintaining prompt templates independently and beginning to duplicate effort or diverge on similar tasks. A single team with one agent service can manage prompts in source control without a registry; the registry pays for itself when it enables cross-team prompt reuse and centralized safety scanning.

Prioritize the runtime retrieval SDK and version pinning over the discovery UI when first building the registry. The most important property for initial adoption is that consuming services can reliably retrieve the right version of a prompt at runtime and that registry unavailability does not cause service failures. The discovery and search features are lower priority than core versioning and retrieval reliability.

# prompts/summarize-doc.yaml - prompt definition for registry publication
name: summarize-doc
version: 2.1.0
description: "Summarizes a document into key points with configurable detail level"
tags: [summarization, document-processing]
author: data-tools-team
model:
  provider: anthropic
  id: claude-3-5-haiku-20241022
  temperature: 0.3
  max_tokens: 1024

variables:
  document:
    type: string
    required: true
    description: "The document text to summarize"
  num_points:
    type: integer
    required: false
    default: 5
    description: "Number of key points to extract (1-10)"
  style:
    type: string
    required: false
    default: "concise"
    enum: [concise, detailed, bullet_only]
    description: "Summarization style"

system_prompt: |
  You are a precise document summarizer. Extract the {{ num_points }} most important
  points from the provided document. Use a {{ style }} style.

  Rules:
  - Extract factual information only
  - Do not infer or extrapolate beyond the document
  - Each point should be self-contained and accurate

user_prompt_template: |
  Please summarize the following document:

  <document>
  {{ document }}
  </document>

eval_suite: evals/summarize-doc-smoke.yaml

# Usage in consuming service — prompt registry SDK
from platform_prompt_registry import PromptRegistry

registry = PromptRegistry(
    registry_url="https://prompt-registry.internal.company.com",
    cache_ttl_seconds=300,   # refresh every 5 minutes
    fallback_cache=True,     # use stale cache if registry unavailable
)

# Pin to a minor version range -- receives patch updates automatically
prompt = registry.get("summarize-doc", version=">=2.1,<3.0")

# Render with required + optional variables
rendered = prompt.render({
    "document": document_text,
    "num_points": 3,
    "style": "concise",
})

# rendered is a dict with {system: str, user: str, model: str, params: dict}
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
response = client.messages.create(
    model=rendered["model"],
    system=rendered["system"],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": rendered["user"]}],
    max_tokens=rendered["params"].get("max_tokens", 1024),
)

# CLI: publish and manage prompts

# Validate a prompt definition before publishing
platform prompts validate prompts/summarize-doc.yaml

# Publish (runs automated pipeline: schema check, smoke eval, safety scan)
platform prompts publish prompts/summarize-doc.yaml

# Output:
# Validating schema... OK
# Running smoke eval (5 cases)... 4/5 passed, score: 0.91... OK
# Safety scan... No injection patterns detected... OK
# Published: summarize-doc@2.1.0

# List versions of a prompt
platform prompts list summarize-doc

# View eval history for a prompt version
platform prompts eval-history summarize-doc@2.1.0

# Deprecate an old version
platform prompts deprecate summarize-doc@1.x --successor 2.0.0

The data-tools team authors a new document summarization prompt for their service. They write the prompt definition YAML specifying the template, variable schema, and pointing to their smoke eval suite. They run the validate command to check the schema locally, then the publish command to trigger the publication pipeline. The pipeline runs in 45 seconds: schema validation passes, 5 of 5 smoke eval cases pass with a score of 0.91, the safety scan finds no injection patterns, and the prompt is published as summarize-doc v2.1.0. They update their service to use the registry SDK pinned to the 2.x minor range and deploy.

Six months later, a second team building a different document processing agent discovers summarize-doc v2.1.0 in the registry through the search interface. Rather than authoring a new prompt from scratch, they adopt it with the detailed style option. When the data-tools team publishes summarize-doc v2.2.0 adding a language variable for multi-language support, the second team's service automatically receives the update because they are pinned to the 2.x minor range. No code change is required.

A security review identifies that an older prompt version, summarize-doc v1.3.0, contains a template pattern susceptible to prompt injection when the document variable contains adversarial input. The platform team uses the registry's active-services metadata to identify that two services still use v1.3.0. They contact both teams with the vulnerability details and a remediation timeline, and also update the safety scanner with a new rule to detect similar patterns in future publications. Both services upgrade to v2.1.0 within the week.

LangSmith and LangChain Hub both provide prompt versioning registries: users can store, version, and share prompts with metadata including model configuration, input variables, and eval scores. LangChain Hub allows teams to pull prompts from the hub into their LangChain applications using the hub.pull function. The internal self-service registry described here adapts this model for an organizational context by adding corporate identity integration, access controls, mandatory safety scanning, and integration with the internal eval runner. The core insight is the same: prompts are first-class versioned artifacts, not just strings in code.
Embedding prompt strings directly in application code instead of using the registry, defeating centralized management and discovery.
Pinning to an exact version rather than a minor version range, so patch fixes require a code deployment to receive.
Not configuring a fallback cache in the registry SDK, so service startup fails if the registry is transiently unavailable.
Publishing without meaningful eval coverage so the publication pipeline smoke eval does not catch quality regressions.
Letting deprecated versions accumulate without notifying the services that still use them, leaving teams on unmaintained versions.
Treating the registry as write-only: not reviewing the active-services metadata to understand the impact of deprecations or breaking changes.
Use the input variable schema to validate all required variables at render time; fail loud rather than passing an incomplete template to the LLM.
Pin consuming services to a minor version range, not an exact version, to receive patch updates automatically.
Include a representative smoke eval suite with each prompt definition; the smoke suite is the primary quality gate for publication.
Populate the active-services metadata accurately by having the registry SDK report prompt usage on initialization; this data is essential for impact assessment before deprecation.
Review the registry's prompt catalog quarterly for stale or redundant prompts; deprecate and remove those that are no longer used.
Use the registry's version history and eval scores as evidence in quality review processes; a prompt with a documented eval history is more trustworthy than one without.

Registry reads are the hot path: every service reads prompts on startup and potentially on a refresh schedule. The registry SDK's local cache is the primary performance mechanism. With a 5-minute TTL cache, most prompt reads are served from memory with zero network latency. The registry itself only needs to handle reads at the frequency of cache expiration across all service instances, not on every request. This makes the registry's read throughput requirements modest even at large fleet sizes.

The publication pipeline is the latency-critical path for the authoring workflow. A pipeline that takes 10 minutes to validate, eval, and publish a prompt slows the iteration loop significantly. Keep the smoke eval suite small at 5 to 10 cases for the automated gate, reserving larger eval suites for manual pre-publication testing. The safety scanner should run in under 5 seconds using static analysis rather than LLM-based judgment. A well-designed publication pipeline completes in under 2 minutes for the automated steps.
Metadata in DB, prompt text in blob store
Cache with TTL, fallback on unavailability
Integrates with eval runner and registry API
Search by tag, task type, model, score
Fast to write, slow to maintain

Prompt injection is the primary security concern for a prompt registry: a published prompt that unsafely interpolates user-controlled input without sanitization can be exploited by users who craft inputs that override the system prompt's instructions. The safety scanner must check for patterns where user-supplied variables are interpolated into the system prompt rather than the user prompt, where the template includes meta-instructions that could be overridden, and where the template is designed to work with unconstrained user input without appropriate framing.

Reliability of the registry is a startup dependency for consuming services. Services that fail to start if the registry is unavailable create a cascading failure risk: a registry outage during a deployment window prevents all services from starting. Mitigate this by implementing the registry SDK's fallback cache and by initializing prompts at startup rather than on first use. The registry itself should be deployed with the same high-availability posture as other critical platform services.
A prompt registry treats prompts as first-class versioned artifacts with metadata, eval scores, and an active-services inventory.
Self-service publication with automated quality gates provides governance without creating a platform team bottleneck.
Consuming services should use SDK version ranges, not exact version pins, to receive patch updates automatically.
The registry SDK must implement a fallback cache so registry unavailability does not cause service startup failures.
Safety scanning at publication time prevents injection-vulnerable prompts from reaching production services.
The active-services metadata is essential operational data: it determines the impact scope of deprecations and breaking changes.
